Design System
🎨Design System
Relowa POC · v0.1
Home / Concepts / Server-authoritative State

Server-authoritative State Transitions

TypeConcept

Why the server, not the client, decides when an auction closes. And why this is non-negotiable for anything touching money.

The bait

Auctions have countdowns: “04:12:33 remaining.” It’s tempting to let the client transition the auction to CLOSING when the timer hits zero. The UI already knows the time. Why not?

The trap

🔴

Three reasons this fails, ranked by severity:

  1. Client clocks lie — NTP drift, user-adjusted system time, DevTools manipulation. A determined bidder can win every auction.
  2. Network races — even with honest clocks, two bids arriving within milliseconds are ambiguous. Only the server can resolve ordering.
  3. Sniping — bidders submit a winning bid 1 second before the buzzer, leaving competitors no time to respond. Auctions degrade to “fastest connection wins.”

1. Client clocks lie

Cause Effect
NTP drift on the user’s device Auction “closes” 30s early
User adjusted their system clock Auction “closes” 5 minutes early — or never
DevTools manipulation Bidder sets close time to 23:59:59 — “too late, you lose”

A determined bidder can win every auction by simply telling their browser the auction closed before the rival’s high bid arrived.

2. Network races

Even with honest clocks, network latency makes it ambiguous which bid arrived “first” if both arrive within a few hundred milliseconds. Client-driven closes can’t resolve this — the server can.

3. Sniping

Without server-side soft-close, sophisticated bidders submit a winning bid 1 second before the buzzer, leaving competitors no time to respond. Auctions degrade to “who has the fastest connection.”

Our pattern

✅

The server, never the client, owns state transitions.

  • Server stores closes_at timestamp at PUBLISH.
  • Client renders countdown by subtracting now() from closes_at.
  • EventBridge Scheduler runs every 30 seconds:
UPDATE tenders SET status = 'closing'
WHERE status = 'published' AND closes_at < now();

The client never sends “close this auction” requests. There is no such endpoint.

Soft-close (anti-sniping)

ℹ️

When a bid arrives within 60s of closes_at: 

UPDATE tenders
SET closes_at = greatest(closes_at, now() + interval '60 seconds')
WHERE id = $1 AND status = 'published';

The auction extends by 60s every time a late bid lands. Equivalent to “the gavel doesn’t fall while bids are still flying.”

Where else this applies

Any state transition driven by time, not user action, must be server-side:

Transition Mechanism
Auction expiry EventBridge Scheduler
Bid window soft-close Trigger on bid INSERT
Escrow auto-release after delivery confirmation timeout EventBridge Scheduler
Idempotency key TTL cleanup EventBridge Scheduler
Tender draft auto-discard after 30 days EventBridge Scheduler

pg_cron vs EventBridge Scheduler

We use both. The decision rule:

pg_cron — pure SQL work

Use when the transition does not need to call an external service.

  • Auction expiry (UPDATE rows)
  • Idempotency key cleanup
  • Materialized view refresh
EventBridge Scheduler — cross-service

Use when the transition requires coordination across service boundaries.

  • Scheduled emails
  • Scheduled webhook retries
  • Scheduled S3 uploads

The line: if the work is pure SQL, pg_cron. If it crosses a service boundary, EventBridge Scheduler.

See Also
Idempotency ADR-0002 — Supabase Realtime Standalone
